utterances: (Soundbite of laughter)
We're talking about the U.S./Israeli relationship with Stephen Walt and Aaron David Miller.
You're listening to Talk of the Nation from NPR News.
And Aaron David Miller, it's not just the U.S.

which is changing administrations; there's to be an election in Israel in February with every possibility that they could have a new - well, certainly a new parliament and probably a new prime minister.
Yeah.
You could also send food - not really send food, but order food through these online organizations that deliver the food to them, but mostly remittances, yes.
Yeah.
First of all, I would correct one thing you said.
I don't think this is something created by the Israelis.
This is a domestic special-interest group in the United States, and that's a perfectly legitimate form of democratic politics.
People in the United States can organize to lobby and push politicians and put pressure on media organizations; that's the way our system of government works.

And I think the reason we have this special relationship with Israel is not, as Aaron said, primarily due to shared values; there are some shared values, but you know, there are lots of countries that share American values and we don't have special relationships with all of them.

We have a special relationship because there's a well-organized set of interest groups here, and I think the unfortunate part is that many of them, particularly the most powerful ones, groups like AIPAC, have advocated a set of policies over the years that have been unintentionally quite harmful to Israel and to the United States alike.

And I think Aaron and I might actually agree on some of those policy questions, but I think unless you get an American president who's willing to confront them - and I might add, as we start to get, I think, much more heterogeneity among Israel supporters, more people realizing that the policies we have followed and the policies Israel has followed had been counterproductive - then I think you do have the potential for a real change that would be good for all of us.
Aaron David Miller?
Well, I would argue: that lobbies lobby in not surprising or stunning.
I mean, read the Constitution.
I mean, this is a system designed by the founders with shared and also separated powers.
The founders may have been suspicions of interests group, but what's happened clearly is that you have a number of very well-organized and affluent lobbies that seek to make their points.
I don't find that so remarkable.
What's remarkable is that presidents don't lead - that's the question - and Americans elect their presidents to lead.
When, in fact, presidents do lead - Nixon, Carter and George H.
W.
Bush - it seems to me we can be successful.
I would disagree with Stephen on one issue, however.
I think Israel is unique in the sense that there's a confluence of factors that come together.

There's no question that the presence of five and a half million American Jews and, I might add, joined by millions of evangelical Christians, who support Israel not just for eschatological reasons, because they believe that Israel right or wrong, correct or incorrect, appears more like America than much of the Arabs in Arab and Muslim world.
That has a profound importance.

If you took away shared values, if you took away that the image in most American minds that Israel is a small country, however powerful it may be, living in a dangerous neighborhood, that it wasn't a democracy, that it wasn't based on liberal, humanistic traditions, then I think no amount of lobbying in the world by a pro-Israeli lobby in the United States could deliver the way the U.S./Israeli relationship has turned out.
The one thing you cannot take away and have the relationship prospered is what I call value affinity, the notion that Israel and America share common values.
That is critical to understanding the nature of this relationship.
Sam, thank you.
I'm sorry, Steve Walt, did you want to come back?
Well, again, I think that there is something in what Aaron just said, but I think there are also some pretty distinct differences between the United States and Israel.
Our values are not completely congruent.
The United States is a liberal democracy, a melting-pot democracy, where people of all religions and ethnicities are supposed to enjoy equal rights.
Israel is not a melting-pot democracy; it's explicitly created as a Jewish state.
And there are other countries around the world who are like that, but there is a difference between their democracy and our democracy.
And of course, Israel is not a democracy for all of the Palestinians under its control.

There's a sharp difference in American values there; yet that's not a difference that gets manifest very often in American political debate, and certainly not something that the average congressman or even the average executive-branch official raises with the Israelis very often, and certainly doesn't complain about in public and doesn't make an issue of real political leverage.
I would only respond, Steve, by saying one thing.

If you took a look at American in 1836, 60 years after the Declaration of Independence was signed, you would find a polity which disenfranchised blacks, or slaves, women; in fact, not all males, white males, could vote.
Israel is 60 years old.
It's in an anomalous position; it will have to work through a number of issues for a young struggling democracy, which took us, I might add, more than 200 years to work through.
After all, we've elected Barack Obama to be the 44th president of the United States.
That's an extraordinary transformational act.
And you're right; Israel is not a democracy in the sense that we are, but it deserves our support nonetheless, within reason, as a consequence of the society that it has become.
Let's get Izzy(ph) on the line, Izzy calling us from West Point.
(Soundbite of silence)
Izzy, are you there?
(Soundbite of silence)
All right, I guess Izzy has left us.
And we should instead go now to Peter, Peter calling us from Berkeley, California.
Hi.
I seem to have about five points here.
(Soundbite of laughter)
(As Dr. Christian Szell) Oh, please don't worry.
I'm not going into that cavity.
That nerve is already dying.
A live, freshly cut nerve is infinitely more sensitive.
So, I'll just drill into a healthy tooth until I reach the pulse.
Unless of course you can tell me that it's safe.
(Soundbite of drill)
Brian Rainville says he used the Freedom of Information Act to find out if the new border station is needed.
I'll try to tick them off fast.
Seems like value affinities has to be expanded and promoted to include everyone in the world, and the main value that should be shared is nonviolent means.

So, let's not use our other values to justify massive violence while condemning out of hand the relatively minor violence, which, of course, has this background of frustration, where the disparity where Israel was created and supported and Palestine wasn't by the international community.
So, that same international community that created and recognized Israel is responsible for cleaning up this mess.
And Israel and the U.S.
with its leverage ought to go to the UN, and the United Nations needs to become what it ought to be: a truly global democracy organization with authority behind it.
And that's tough; that's a big change in the world.
So, this is birth pangs of a greater world.
Well, Peter...
We should learn to fight fire with water.
All right, let's say stop on those points.
The United Nations, Stephen Walt, is that going to an effective venue?
It's hard to imagine the United States doing anything.
Yeah, I fear not.
I think Aaron and I, in our own fashion, are trying to work on the Israeli/Palestinian conflict here.
I think adding to that the reform of the United Nations and turning it into a truly effective institution for global governance is too ambitious even for us.
And on the shared values we're getting a lot of emails on this as well.
Here's one from Hassan in California: What shared values?
Israel does not have a constitution.
It has two sets of laws, one covering Jews and one covering non-Jews, not only in the occupied territories, but also inside Israel.
Israel demolishes homes and villages of Bedouin in the Negev and Arabs in the Galilee to Judaize(ph) the entire country.
President Carter correctly called this apartheid.
And well, you yourself drew a comparison to slavery, not, obviously, in a direct analogy.
That was to you.

I mean, you know, I think, as I pointed out and I'm not going to defend the anomalies in the Israeli democratic polity because there are many, and the Israelis are going to have to find a way to work through them, including the reality that a million Israeli citizens - roughly 20 percent of the population - suffer systemic, social and economic discrimination that as Palestinian citizens of Israel or Israeli-Arabs.

But nonetheless, you can probably count on one hand the number of emerging democracies who actually change power regularly according to democratic form and format, surrender power, in which there's a contract between the governed and the governing.

I think in that particular case, it's extremely important to understand that that shared value, fellow democratic polities abroad, particularly ones that are under tremendous stress - I mean, we have non-predatory neighbors to our north and south and fish to our east and west.
We don't understand the existential dilemma, not just of the Israelis, but the small tribes including the Arabs and the Palestinians that live in that part of the world.

So, I think I believe on balance -and no country is perfect - that there is a case for shared values, and it's one that the vast majority of the American public, when they focus on this issue, appears willing to support.
Peter, thanks very much.
Well, thank you.
All right.
Bye-bye.
Stephen Walt, let me follow up with you.
As a practical matter, what do you foresee?
We do have a change of administration.

During the presidential election campaign, Barack Obama went to appear before the AIPAC and said that he was going to support Israel, the same as every other presidential candidate has vowed to do so in my lifetime, to my memory.
Do you think things will change?
I wish I could say they were.
I don't think so.

I think part of it is that Obama is going to have an enormous amount on his plate already, with the situation in the American economy, with the problems we still face in Iraq, with the continued problems in Afghanistan, with Pakistan and India and elsewhere.
So, he's got a very full agenda, and he's going to be told by a lot of his advisers that Israel/Palestine is just too hard.
Now, it's possible that what's happening in Gaza today is going to force that to the front of his agenda right from the very beginning, and you might see more engagement.
The questions are, are we going to get an engagement that's really affective here?
And I think that's going to involve him talking very tough in private to Israeli interlocutors and to appointing a special envoy that has real credibility with both sides there.

It's going to involve making some public statements similar to, say, the speech that Secretary of State James Baker made to AIPAC back in the early '90s, where he said it was time to give up the dream of greater Israel.
That's going to involve engaging with the more progressive parts of the American Jewish community and other groups that are supportive of Israel but not blindly supportive.
And you can then imagine a series of other steps that could be taken designed to convey it.
Last but not the least, I think the United States has to lay out, as President Clinton did in his last month in office, his own - our own vision of what a just settlement is.
That's not dictating a settlement to the parties, but it's laying out what we think a fair outcome is here.
And again, we're not doing that in anti-Israeli spirit; we're doing that in a pro-Israeli spirit.

We're recognizing that, as Prime Minister Ehud Olmert said a year or so ago, that if the two-state solution fails, Israel will face a South Africa style struggle, and then he added, if that happens, the state of Israel is finished.

So, in a funny way, if President Obama is a true friend of Israel, he will actually adopt a more evenhanded position and use the leverage at his disposal to try and bring about a solution that would be in everybody's best interest.
Father James Martin wrote the book ""My Life with the Saints,"" and he wants to remind the faithful at the beginning of Lent that joy is one of the upsides of being religious.
And he feels that more people need to remember that even God has a sense of humor, he thinks.
Actually, Neal, that, I think, is a critical point, because leadership on all sides is going to be absolutely essential in order to make anything happen.
And here's where I think I'm very pessimistic.

You have a Palestinian national movement that is dysfunctional and broken and divided; you have an Israeli government that has shown us three Israeli prime ministers, none of whom have been terribly successful.
And that leaves an American president - and I agree with Steve Walt on this - an American president who understands that governing is about choosing.
He's not the war president; he's not the peace president; he's the fix-America's-broken-house president.

I voted for Barack Obama, and I'll vote for him again in 2012 whether or not he engages on the Arab/Israeli issue, because his real mandate - and this is where Steve Walt, I think, is absolutely right - his real mandate is to fix this economy, keep America secure and provide some measure of hope that the future that he's inheriting will be brighter four years from now.
So, governing is about choosing.
He'll manage this.
He has a very able secretary of State who will probably create an empire of process with very talented, very smart envoys.
Whether or not he's prepared to be tough, smart and fair, I guess we're just going to have to wait and see.
Let me ask you also another point.
You talked about the U.S.
influence on Israel and the difficulty of actually employing those levers.
Does Israel - sometimes people have the impression that Israel clears its plans in advance with the United States, provides information ahead of time so that it can get U.S.
support.
Is that always the case?
Not always.
I think the Israelis don't want to be crosswise(ph) with us, particularly when it concerns consequential acts they might take.
For example, a military strike on Iran; they can't do that without American support, at least acquiescence.
So, that's clearly an example of something they'd have to come to us with no - by and large, Israel wants to be in the same boat as America.
My only point is, let's try to make sure that boat isn't leaking and that it essentially has a port, a good navigator and a reasonably calm sea so that we can make some progress.
And I do believe, despite all of my skepticism, that Barack Obama has the necessary skills, the smarts, the discretion and the toughness to actually be a good negotiator.
He simply has to make one decision.
Does he believe the pursuit of Arab/Israeli peace is a high priority for the United States?
If he does, good things can happen.
And Stephen Walt, do you see that there is a - at least the beginnings of a lobby group to promote the Palestinians side, the Arab side, if you will?
Well, there are some rather politically weak groups that attempt to do that.
I think what is more interesting is the growing emergence of, as I said before, heterogeneity within the ranks of Israel supporters; the new group, J Street, for example, would be an example of that.

And I think we also see just a changed discourse here in the United States, not completely, not as much in the mainstream media, but elsewhere people beginning to recognize that a more open discussion of this issue is ultimately going to lead to better policy from the United States and hopefully a better outcome over there.
But I'm not too confident.
Thanks to you both.
Stephen Walt, a professor of international relations at Harvard's Kennedy School of Government, co-author of ""The Israel Lobby and U.S.
Foreign Policy,"" with us today from member station WBUR in Boston.

Here in Studio 3A in Washington, Aaron David Miller, a former adviser to six secretaries of State on Arab/Israeli negotiations, the author of ""The Much Too Promised Land."" Thanks very much to you both.
When we come back, the great search for an empty for a sofa for the inauguration and how to call a friend you haven't talked to for 10 years.
This is NPR News.
The federal government is also spending hundreds of millions of dollars to beef up security along the northern border with Canada.
Both the Department of Homeland Security is being criticized for planning expensive projects that may not be needed.
In Vermont, for example, the government plans to seize a farmer's land to build a new $5 million border post on a quiet country road.
The local community is fiercely opposed to the project and the state's congressional delegation says the government should scrap it.
More from Vermont public radio's John Dillon.
